Official name (+source): ビオセボン・ジャポン株式会社 (Bio c' Bon Japon Co., Ltd.) — bio-c-bon.jp/company/
Instagram: Not found in verified public sources
Est. company size: 236 employees reported by data aggregator (unverified) — pitchbook.com; 24 stores in Tokyo area as of reporting reviewed (2026)
Primary customers: Retail consumers in Tokyo (and reportedly Kanagawa) seeking certified organic food and household products
Year founded: Japan entity established June 2016 as joint venture between Aeon Co. Ltd. and Marne & Finance Europe — aeon.info
Ownership status: Private joint venture; Aeon took 20% stake in French parent Dec 2018; French parent Bio c'Bon acquired by Carrefour 2020-2021 (Japan entity's post-acquisition ownership not confirmed) — carrefour.com; autoritedelaconcurrence.fr
Core model: Organic supermarket chain (1,000+ certified-organic lines) with in-store bulk/refill 'pick-your-own' stations for nuts, dried fruit, grains and lentils at several Tokyo locations — bio-c-bon.jp; savvytokyo.com
Categories: Consumer Goods; Refill Stations; Organic Retail
Key partners / customers: Aeon Co. Ltd. (JV partner/shareholder); Marne & Finance Europe (French chain owner/JV co-founder); Carrefour (French parent acquirer, 2020-2021) — aeon.info; carrefour.com
Impact metrics: Not publicly available (no quantified refill-station volume, plastic-reduction or waste-diversion metric found in verified sources)
Verification notes: Refill/bulk stations are a secondary in-store feature within a conventional organic-supermarket business, not the core model. Ownership of the Japan entity following Carrefour's 2020-2021 acquisition of the French parent brand is not confirmed in public sources -- flag for follow-up.

Official name (+source): bio-home, brand of Lam Soon Singapore Pte Ltd — biohomecares.com; lamsoon.com
Instagram: Not found in verified public sources
Est. company size: Not found in verified public sources as a standalone figure; parent Lam Soon Group is a multi-country FMCG group (Singapore, Malaysia, Thailand, Vietnam, Hong Kong) — lamsoon.com
Primary customers: Singapore households/retail consumers via supermarkets and e-commerce; institutional/campus users via refill-station pilot
Year founded: Brand created in response to 2007 'Green Awakening' per company materials; exact bio-home launch year not confirmed in verified sources. Parent Lam Soon founded 1929, incorporated 1950 — lamsoon.com; brandzasia.com
Ownership status: Private; brand of Lam Soon Singapore Pte Ltd, part of Lam Soon Group (linked to Hong Kong-listed Lam Soon (Hong Kong) Limited group) — lamsoon.com
Core model: Manufacturer-brand model: plant-based biodegradable household cleaning products sold via conventional retail (bottled), plus a 2025 pilot automated refill/dispensing station (with Ecoworks) letting users refill their own containers — biohomecares.com; futr.sg
Categories: Consumer Goods; Eco-Friendly Household Products; Refill Systems
Key partners / customers: Ecoworks (refill-station pilot partner, 2025); Singapore Environment Council / Singapore Green Labelling Scheme (certification); NTUC FairPrice, Kohepets, Good Dog People, Grocery Owl, Amazon Singapore (retail distribution) — futr.sg; sgls.sec.org.sg
Impact metrics: Ecoworks/bio-home refill pilot reported to save users up to 37% vs. buying pre-bottled product (2025); no independently audited plastic/waste-reduction figures found — futr.sg. Self-reported cost-saving claim, not an audited environmental metric.
Verification notes: Exact bio-home brand launch year not confirmed (only 'post-2007' framing found); refill-station model is a single-site pilot as of the source reviewed, not yet a national program -- distinguish from bio-home's core conventional bottled-product business.

Official name (+source): 'Retornáveis' / 'Coca-Cola Retornável' packaging program of The Coca-Cola Company, implemented by licensed Brazilian bottling partners — coca-cola.com/br/pt/offerings/retornaveis
Instagram: Not found in verified public sources
Est. company size: Not applicable — program operated by multiple Coca-Cola bottling franchisees in Brazil (incl. Coca-Cola FEMSA Brasil, Solar Coca-Cola); no standalone headcount
Primary customers: Brazilian consumers, primarily via small independent retailers ('padarias'/mercadinhos'), bars/restaurants, and some supermarkets
Year founded: No discrete founding date -- returnable bottles are a long-standing legacy format in Brazil; global 'World Without Waste' packaging strategy announced 2018; associated SustentaPET recycling initiative created 2019 by Coca-Cola FEMSA Brasil
Ownership status: Program of The Coca-Cola Company (NYSE: KO), implemented in Brazil via independently owned licensed bottling franchisees — cocacolabrasil.com.br
Core model: Returnable glass (200/290/1000/1250ml) and RefPET (1500/2000ml) bottles across Coca-Cola, Sprite, Fanta, Del Valle, Guaraná Jesus, Kuat, Tuchaua brands; consumers pay full price plus deposit-like bottle cost first purchase, then liquid-only price on return; PET refilled ~25x, glass refilled ~50x — coca-cola.com; ad-hoc-news.de
Geographic scope: Brazil (national; part of wider Latin America returnable strategy)
Categories: Consumer Goods; Reusable Packaging; Deposit Return
Key partners / customers: Implemented via Coca-Cola FEMSA Brasil and Solar Coca-Cola bottling franchises; regional bottler brands (Refrescos Bandeirantes, Brasal Refrigerantes, Sorocaba Refrescos) market the campaign locally — refrescosbandeirantes.com.br; brasal.com.br
Impact metrics: Returnables ~20% of Coca-Cola's Brazil sales volume vs. ~34% across Latin America (trade press, 2024); SustentaPET (since 2019, Coca-Cola FEMSA Brasil) reports 3+ billion PET bottles recycled (recycling, not reuse, metric); reporting indicates Coca-Cola's global 25%-by-2030 reusable-packaging pledge was later removed from parts of public company communications — ad-hoc-news.de; fastcompanybrasil.com. Mix of trade-press and company-sourced figures, not independently audited.
Verification notes: This is a packaging/marketing program run by multiple independent Brazilian bottling franchisees under The Coca-Cola Company brand, not a single standalone company -- 'official name' and 'ownership' fields reflect that structure. Trade press reports the parent company's global reuse commitment language was scaled back in late 2023/2024, raising questions about target durability; no updated public target found to replace it.

Official name (+source): Morocco PET Circularity Pilot (bottle-to-bottle pilot), part of the SWITCH2CE Project, led by Equatorial Coca-Cola Bottling Company (ECCBC) with UNIDO — switchtocircular.eu; eccbc.com/esg-report/packaging-2024/
Website: Not applicable (no dedicated project website; see switchtocircular.eu/value-chains/plastic-packaging)
Instagram: Not found in verified public sources
Est. company size: Not applicable -- multi-stakeholder pilot project, not a company
Primary customers: Waste pickers/collector communities (organized by Roots for Sustainability), Moroccan retail/residential PET collection points; ECCBC as industrial off-taker of recycled PET
Year founded: Structured as a '3-year initiative'; exact signing date of partners' agreement not confirmed in verified sources; related IFC/ECCBC partnership announced April 2023 — ifc.org
Ownership status: Not applicable — multi-stakeholder pilot led by ECCBC (privately held Coca-Cola bottling franchise) with UNIDO (UN specialized agency); funded by EU and Government of Finland via SWITCH2CE — switchtocircular.eu
Core model: Three-year industrial pilot building a scalable, financially viable bottle-to-bottle PET collection and recycling system: household-level collection (Roots for Sustainability), point-of-sale collection and transport (Right Execution Daily), recycling (La Générale du Recyclage), business-case/financial modelling (ThirdWay Partners) — switchtocircular.eu
Categories: Beverage Packaging; Material Recovery; Industrial Reuse
Key partners / customers: UNIDO; European Union and Government of Finland (SWITCH2CE co-funders); La Générale du Recyclage (LGR); Roots for Sustainability (R4S); Right Execution Daily (RED); ThirdWay Partners (TWP); International Finance Corporation (IFC, related loan); Casablanca-Settat Regional Council; Circle Economy, Chatham House, European Investment Bank cited as ESMP partner organizations — switchtocircular.eu; ifc.org
Impact metrics: ECCBC's 2024 ESG report states the pilot 'continued to make strong progress' and 'efforts have been scaled' during the year, but no tonnage of PET collected/recycled, collector numbers, or rPET volume incorporated into bottles was disclosed — eccbc.com/esg-report/packaging-2024/. No independently quantified metric found.
Verification notes: This is a recycling/material-recovery pilot (bottle-to-bottle rPET), not a direct reuse programme in the sense of extending a package's life through repeated reuse -- distinguish from Coca-Cola's separate returnable-bottle reuse programs. No dedicated project website exists; documented via SWITCH2CE program pages and ECCBC's own ESG report.

Official name (+source): Eco Now (trading name; online as 'eco now, california') — econowca.com
Primary customers: Southern California consumers (Orange County, formerly Inland Empire) seeking zero-waste and package-free household/personal-care/grocery products
Year founded: 2018, founded by Thea Merritt (began at Placentia farmers' market) — voyagela.com; letsgozerowaste.com
Ownership status: Privately held small/independent business, founder-run (Thea Merritt) — voyagela.com
Core model: Bulk refill stations (shampoo, home cleaning, hygiene products) sold by weight/volume plus sale of handmade/sourced reusable goods (produce bags, beeswax wraps, bulk-food bags); in-store composting education and upcycling assistance — econowca.com; letsgozerowaste.com
Geographic scope: United States -- Southern California (Orange County: Anaheim, Costa Mesa, formerly Laguna Beach; formerly Riverside/Inland Empire)
Categories: Consumer Goods; Refillable Products; Zero Waste Retail
Key partners / customers: Not publicly available (no named corporate, NGO or brand partnerships found in public sources)
Impact metrics: Not publicly available (no independently verified figures for plastic avoided, refills completed, or waste diverted found in company or press sources)
Verification notes: Multiple location closures/consolidation documented: Riverside store (opened June 2021) listed closed; Laguna Beach store (opened 2022) closed ~Sept 1 2025; Costa Mesa flagship reported temporarily closed for expansion with reopening cited variously as 2026/2027 in aggregator listings; Anaheim location (opened 2020) still listed as operating in sources reviewed. Status should be read as 'active but contracting' rather than stable multi-site operation.
